story.title: The Peel Chain — Linear Layering
story.narrative: This story traces a classic **peel chain** — a money-layering pattern where illicit funds flow through a linear sequence of transactions, each passing the balance forward to the next node. Peel chains are structurally simple (in-degree = 1, out-degree = 1 for intermediate nodes), making them easy to detect but still widely used in the wild. Our seed node is part of a 21-hop chain where every node is flagged as illicit, demonstrating a high-confidence laundering pipeline.

steps.title: Identify the seed
steps.description: Start at transaction 10000476 — an illicit node flagged by the Elliptic++ labels. It has in-degree = 1 and out-degree = 1, meaning it simply forwards funds to the next hop. This is the hallmark of a peel chain intermediate node.

steps.title: Trace the first few hops
steps.description: Follow the downstream edge from 10000476 through 163822045 and 163822047. Each intermediate node passes the full balance forward with no branching. All three are illicit, forming the start of a clear layering pipeline.

steps.title: The full chain
steps.description: Extending the trace reveals 20 consecutive illicit nodes in a straight line. No licit nodes mix in — this is a clean peel chain where every hop is labeled class = 1 (illicit). Such chains are EASY cases because the structural signal (degree-2 linear path) combined with label consistency leaves no ambiguity.

steps.title: Chain termination
steps.description: The chain eventually terminates when a node fans out to multiple recipients or hits a dead end. At that point the laundered funds are either consolidated for cash-out or dispersed to avoid further tracking. The structural endpoint marks the end of the peel chain.
